coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and bismuth-doped rice husk-derived carbon quantum dots for dye degradation and heavy metal removal
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Chung Hui, Khee
Lun Ang, Wei
Soraya Sambudi, Nonni
coredata.dc:description: In this study, heteroatoms doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized using rice husk as a biowaste precursor. The effects of nitrogen and bismuth doping are investigated by adjusting the amount of ethylenediamine (EDA) as the nitrogen source, and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate as the bismuth source. HRTEM analysis depicted the formation of spherical carbon quantum dots, with a size of less than 10 nm. FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed the presence of essential functional groups and chemical composition in both of the doped carbon quantum dots. UV–Vis spectra showed peaks which are associated to the electronic transition of the C = C bonding. The addition of EDA and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate tuned the fluorescence intensity and shifted the emission wavelength from the blue region to green one. The performance of the doped rice husk derived carbon quantum dots (RHCQDs) was evaluated via photodegradation of the methylene blue (MB), under a 300 W Xenon lamp. as well as via copper (II) removal. N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) demonstrated the highest MB degradation performance, at 72.16% and 68.91%, respectively. The degradation of MB of the two doped RHCQDs fitted with the pseudo-second kinetic model. The doped RHCQDs were then tested for copper (II) removal, obtaining removal performance of 56.23% and 33.13%, respectively. The results suggest that the Cu (II) adsorption onto the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs agrees with the pseudo-second kinetic model and the Langmuir isotherm, with maximum adsorption capacity of 5.73 and 4.08 mg/g, respectively.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and bismuth-doped rice husk-derived carbon quantum dots for dye degradation and heavy metal removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, heteroatoms doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized using rice husk as a biowaste precursor. The effects of nitrogen and bismuth doping are investigated by adjusting the amount of ethylenediamine (EDA) as the nitrogen source, and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate as the bismuth source. HRTEM analysis depicted the formation of spherical carbon quantum dots, with a size of less than 10 nm. FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed the presence of essential functional groups and chemical composition in both of the doped carbon quantum dots. UV–Vis spectra showed peaks which are associated to the electronic transition of the C = C bonding. The addition of EDA and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate tuned the fluorescence intensity and shifted the emission wavelength from the blue region to green one. The performance of the doped rice husk derived carbon quantum dots (RHCQDs) was evaluated via photodegradation of the methylene blue (MB), under a 300 W Xenon lamp. as well as via copper (II) removal. N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) demonstrated the highest MB degradation performance, at 72.16% and 68.91%, respectively. The degradation of MB of the two doped RHCQDs fitted with the pseudo-second kinetic model. The doped RHCQDs were then tested for copper (II) removal, obtaining removal performance of 56.23% and 33.13%, respectively. The results suggest that the Cu (II) adsorption onto the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs agrees with the pseudo-second kinetic model and the Langmuir isotherm, with maximum adsorption capacity of 5.73 and 4.08 mg/g, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Aquatic pollution associated with heavy metal- and dye-contaminated wastewater has become a global issue, bringing about negative effects, not only to the human beings, but also to the aquatic organisms. The toxic and carcinogenic nature of dyes and heavy metals will damage the organs and systems of aquatic organisms, eventually disrupting the food chain, and destroying the aquatic ecosystem [1,2]. There are many methods available for the removal of dyes and heavy metals from the aqueous environment, such as adsorption, membrane technology, Fenton-oxidation, ozonation, biological treatment, and photocatalysis. Photocatalysis and adsorption processes are normally preferable due to its process simplicity, and low toxicity [3,4]. Recently, the utilization of fluorescent nanomaterials in the removal of contaminants has become an emerging topic among researchers. The mechanism of contaminants removal normally happens through the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent nanomaterials, whereby the interaction between contaminants and functional groups of the fluorescent nanomaterial will change the energy states of the material and increase its recombination in the fluorescent nanomaterial [5–7]. Fluorescent quenching can also occur due to non-radiative electron transfer from an excited state in the fluorescent material, to the orbitals of the contaminant [8]. Ever since the first discovery in the year 2004 by Xu et al. from the purification of single wall carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained much attention among researchers [9]. CQDs are novel small carbon nanoparticles which contain surface functional groups, such as sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon, oxygen, carboxyl, and hydroxyl bonding, which allows them to have unique fluorescent properties [10]. Researchers have shown interest in exploring the potential applications of CQDs, due to their outstanding fluorescence properties, facile synthesis, low cost, chemical inertness, low toxicity, good solubility, and good photochemical stability [11]. CQDs have been proved to be outstanding materials in numerous applications such as bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, photocatalysis, metal detection as well as metal removal.

The method of synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) can be divided into top-down approaches, and bottom-up approaches. Top-down approaches involve methods which can derive carbon dots from large carbon molecules, such as activated carbon and graphite carbon nanorods [11,12]. On the other hand, bottom-up approaches are methods that extract carbon dots from carbon-containing precursors, such as citric acid, table sugar, glucose, fruits, vegetables etc [11,12]. Top-down approaches like ultrasonic-assisted synthesis, laser-assisted synthesis, and chemical oxidation, are usually not preferred due to disadvantages, such as high processing cost, complex procedures, and energy intensive [13–15]. Bottom up approaches, especially hydrothermal method, are recommended in the synthesis of CQDs owing to its low cost, controlled morphology, and facile synthesis method [16]. The utilization of biowaste as green carbon source in CQDs synthesis not only is a friendly approach towards the reduction of waste in the environment, but also serves as an alternative to synthetic carbon sources used for CQDs synthesis. In this study, rice husks, which are agricultural waste from the rice-milling process were chosen as precursors for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. The annual production of rice husks was 156 million tons in 2018 [17]. The huge production of rice gives rise to the large amount of agricultural waste, of which, rice husk is one of them. In other words, the utilization of rice husk can convert the low-valued rice husk waste to that of a high-valued feedstock for CQDs. Apart from that, the high carbon content of 30–50% in the rice husks is also a reason for selecting rice husks as a biowaste precursor in the synthesis of CQDs [18,19].

In recent years, studies have been focused on developing heteroatom doped-CQDs. Heteroatom doping is a surface modification process in CQDs, in which the electrons’ transfer and optical properties of the carbon dots will be altered and enhanced by introducing metal and non-metal atom as support to the CQDs [20]. Heteroatom doping of CQDs enable the enlargement of the band gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for excitation, thus shifting the fluorescence from blue to red emission [20]. Therefore, by using different amounts of doping reagents, the photoluminescence and band gap of the CQDs can be tuned, allowing the CQDs to depict their distinct light emission properties. Non-metal based doping showed a great potential in the application of biosensing, degradation of pollutant and bioimaging. Among all heteroatom dopants, nitrogen (N) doping is the most used method to improve the photophysical and optical properties of carbon dots, due to its similar size, which is similar to that of the carbon atom [21,22]. Apart from that, N-doping has also proven its effectiveness in enhancing the performance of CQDs in various applications. Hu et al. synthesized N-doped carbon dots from across four different amines, with the highest quantum yield reaching 0.29, and the highest photodegradation efficiency of the indigo-carmine reaching 97% after 2 h [23]. In the field of metal removal, Issa et al. synthesized N-doped carbon nanodots from carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) for copper (II) removal, and the removal efficiency was 83% in just 30 min [24]. Zainal Abidin et al. synthesized amino functionalized carbon quantum dots from rice husks for cadmium removal, and the removal percentage was over 70%, in just 20 min [19].

Compared to non-metal doping, studies on metal-doping of CQDs are still limited, especially in the field of photocatalysis. In this study, bismuth (Bi) was selected as the metal dopant to enhance the photoluminescence of the CQDs. Bismuth (Bi) is a pentavalent transition metal, that is non-toxic, and has low thermal conductivity [25]. Bismuth, when deposited on the surface of another material, is proven to enhance its optical, electrical and chemical properties [25]. In the study by Wang et al., the bismuth doped carbon quantum dots were proven to have good electron transfer properties compared to other metal dopants, thus helping in promoting the hydrogen evolution activity of the paired photocatalyst [26].

To date, there are limited studies regarding the comparison of non-metal doped CQDs, and metal doped CQDs, in terms of their characterization and performance. Thus, in this paper, the authors utilized rice husks as bio-based precursor for the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs. Following that, the rice husk derived carbon dots (RHCQDs) were doped with nitrogen (N) and bismuth (Bi) precursors, respectively. The effects of the amount of the dopant used in the hydrothermal synthesis on the properties of RHCQDs were explored in detail. Subsequently, the performance of both the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs in dye degradation and heavy metal removal were also investigated. Methylene blue (MB) was used as a baseline dye, whereas copper (II) solution was used for the metal removal by the doped RHCQDs.

Rice husks for use as biowaste precursor in the synthesis of carbon quantum dots were obtained from the Perak Agricultural Department, Malaysia. Hydrochloric acid (37%, CAT No: H/1200/PC17) and sodium hydroxide (CAS No: 1310-73-2) were purchased from Fischer Scientific, U.K. Ethylenediamine (CAS-No: 107-15-3) and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (CAS-No: 10035-06-0) were purchased from Merck and Acros Organics, respectively. Methylene blue (CAS-No: 61-73-4) and copper (II) sulphate pentahydrate (CAS-No: 7758-99-8) were purchased from Bendosen Laboratory Chemicals. Sodium chloride (CAS-No: 7647-14-5) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) from PURELAB flex was utilized throughout the experiment for washing and dilution purposes.

The collected rice husks were washed with DI water to remove the large impurities in the rice husks. The rice husks were then blended into fine particles using a countertop blender. The fine rice husks were then pretreated with 0.1 M hydrochloric acid to remove the impurities on the surface and washed with DI water repeatedly until the pH of the rice husks reached neutral. Following that, the rice husks were dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C. EDA with a volume variation of between 5 and 20 vol%, and bismuth nitrate pentahydrate with mass variation of between 1 and 5 wt%, were used as doping reagents for N-doping and Bi-doping, respectively. 190 °C was selected as the hydrothermal temperature for the synthesis of RHCQDs according to several academic studies related to the synthesis of rice husk-derived carbon quantum dots [19,27]. 0.5 g of rice husks, 50 mL DI water, and varied amount of doping reagents were added to the Teflon-lined autoclave to undergo hydrothermal process for 4 h. The mixture was then cooled to room temperature before undergoing vacuum filtration to remove the unwanted solid in the mixture. The liquid carbon quantum dots were dialyzed against DI water in a Visking tubing (Cellulose, pore size 2.5 nm) overnight, to further remove the residual precursors. The obtained carbon quantum dots samples were labelled as N-RHCQDs (5 vol%), N-RHCQDs (10 vol%), N-RHCQDs (20 vol%), Bi-RHCQDs (1 wt%), Bi-RHCQDs (3 wt%), and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%).

The morphology of the doped RHCQDs was investigated using Tecnai G2 20 S-Twin TEM. The particle size and lattice spacing of the doped RHCQDs were analyzed using the Image J software. Fourier-Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis was obtained using the Perkin Elmer IR spectrophotometer from wavenumber of 500–4000 cm−1. The elemental compositions of the RHCQDs samples were determined using the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (XPS). The UV–Vis analysis of the RHCQDs was performed using the Agilent Cary Series UV–Vis spectrophotometer in the range of 200–800 nm. Lastly, the photoluminescence (PL) of the prepared samples were verified using the Edinburgh Instrument FLS920 photoluminescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

The pH at point zero charge (pHPZC) of the N-RCHQDs and Bi-RHCQDs were determined using salt addition method. The salt addition method was done by adding similar amounts of materials to a set of solutions having similar ionic charges at different pH values [28,29]. Across a series of 500 mL beakers, 5 mL of doped RHCQDs samples were added into 300 mL of 1 M NaCl solution. The pH was adjusted using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH as required, to get pH range of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12. The initial pH of all samples were then recorded. Subsequently, the samples were stirred for 24 h at 200 rpm. Following that, the final pH of the samples were then measured. The pHPZC was evaluated from a plot of the final pH against the initial pH.

The photocatalytic activity of the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs samples were evaluated through degradation of MB in aqueous solution under a 300 W Xenon lamp. The volume ratio of the RHCQDs: MB was set at 1:3. The mixture of the RHCQDs and MB (10 ppm) was left in dark for 30 min to achieve an equilibrium. A sample at 5.0 mL was pipetted at different time intervals and centrifuged to remove the RHCQDs. The absorbance of the obtained samples was analyzed using the Spectroquant Prove 600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (λ = 664.5 nm) to evaluate the degradation rate of the MB dye. The effect of the initial concentration on the degradation rate of the MB dye was studied by varying the concentration of the MB dye at 5 ppm, 10 ppm and 15 ppm. On the other hand, the effect of the solution’s pH toward the degradation rate of MB dye was investigated using variations of 2.5, 6.5, and 12.5, by adjusting the pH using 0.1 M HCl, or NaOH.

The copper (II) ions adsorption onto the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs samples were carried out with batch experiments. 10 ppm of copper solution was firstly prepared by dissolving a suitable amount of copper (II) sulphate pentahydrate in DI water. For the batch adsorption experiment, the volume ratio of the RHCQDs to the copper solution was set at 1:3. The mixture of the RHCQDs and 10 ppm copper solution was stirred at a speed of 200 rpm. At a contact time of 5 min, 10 min, 15 min, 30 min, 60 min, 120 min, and 240 min, 15 mL of the liquid sample was collected and analyzed using the Agilent 240 FS atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS). The adsorption capacity and removal performance of the Cu (II) ions were calculated using the following equations:

The best duration of the copper (II) adsorption or contact time was used for the subsequent studies. The influence of the initial concentration of the Cu (II) solution on the adsorption of Cu (II) onto the RHCQDs was studied using variations of 10 ppm, 25 ppm, 50 ppm, and 100 ppm. The impact of the initial pH of the Cu (II) solution on the adsorption of the Cu (II) onto the RHCQDs was also investigated using variations in the pH, at 2.5, 6.6, and 12.3, by adding 0.1 M HCl or NaOH.

HRTEM analysis proved the successful formation of carbon quantum dots. Fig. 1 shows that the doped RHCQDs exhibited a spherical morphology. All the doped RHCQDs existed in amorphous state. The N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) had an average particle diameter of 8.296 ± 1.428 and 4.892 ± 1.050 nm, respectively. Both the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs had a lattice spacing of 0.32 and 0.36 nm, respectively. The dots existed in large, agglomerated cluster. The agglomeration might be due to the high surface energy of the CQDs that led to the strong attraction between the nanoparticles [30].

FTIR analysis confirmed the functional groups present in the doped RHCQDs. Fig. 2 illustrates the FTIR spectra of all the doped RHCQDs samples. Infrared analysis detected a strong absorption at 3300 cm−1, which corresponded to the O–H stretching [31]. Apart from that, C = O bonding was found at 1640 cm−1 for all the synthesized RHCQDs [32]. For the N-RHCQDs, the amino functional groups, N–H bending and N–H stretching, were observed at 1490 cm−1 and 2872 cm−1 respectively [33]. Small peaks were detected at 737 cm−1 in the N-RHCQDs, which corresponded to the C–H bending of the N-RHCQDs [34]. As for the Bi-RHCQDs, the Bi element was not detectable owing to the small amount being used.

X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (XPS) confirmed the elemental compositions and chemical bonding states in the doped RHCQDs samples. The survey spectra in Fig. 3 showed three main peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s, at binding energies of 285, 400, and 500 eV, respectively. The peaks proved the existence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in the two doped carbon quantum dots. For the N-RHCQDs, it can be observed that the peak intensities of the N1s were clearly greater than that of the Bi-RHCQDs. For the Bi-RHCQDs, a small peak of Bi4f was found at a binding energy of 165 eV, indicating the presence of the bismuth element in Bi-RHCQDs in a very small amount. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the N-RHCQDs were presented in Fig. 3 (b-d), and in Fig. 3 (e-h) for the Bi-RHCQDs. From the high-resolution C1s spectra of N-RHCQDs (10 vol%), essential functional groups presented in the CQDs were at 284.96, 286.53, 287.57, 288.96, and 289.76 eV, which corresponded to the C–C, C-N, and C = O bonding, respectively [35,36]. The N1s high-resolution spectra showed the presence of C-N, C = N, and N = O bonds, at 399.97, 401.68 and 406.47 eV, respectively [36]. The peaks at 532.68, 533.62, and 534.18 eV in the high resolution O1s spectra were attributed to binding of C-O, C-OH, and O = C-O, respectively [6].

On the other hand, for the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%), the high-resolution spectra of the C1s also detected the presence of essential carbon features of the CQDs at 284.70, 286.12, and 288.14 eV, which corresponded to the C–C, C-N, and C = O bindings, respectively [36–38]. The high-resolution spectra of the N1s showed the presence of C-N bonding in the CQDs at a binding energy of 400.29 eV [36]. The peaks at 531.07, 532.68, and 533.32 eV of the high-resolution spectra of the O1s are corresponded to C = O, C-O and O = C-O bonds, respectively [39]. Finally, in the high resolution spectra of the Bi4f5/2, a small peak at a binding energy of 164.50 eV proves presence of the Bi3+ ion in the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) sample [40]. This is supported by the study by Jamila et al. that confirms the successful doping of bismuth element in carbon quantum dots, even though the XPS spectrum shows small and broad Bi4f peak which is due to weak signal [41].

Table 1 shows the elemental composition of the two samples obtained from the XPS analysis. The nitrogen content was higher in the N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) sample. The presence of nitrogen associating bonds, and the higher nitrogen content in the sample, proved that nitrogen was successfully doped onto the RHCQDs. As for the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) sample, bismuth was present in the sample at a low atomic percentage of 0.05%, indicating the successful doping of the element onto the RHCQDs. There were some nitrogen elements detected in the Bi-RHCQDs sample, which could be attributed to the nitrogen content of the rice husk precursor itself [19].

Fig. 4 (a) shows the UV–Vis spectra of all the synthesized carbon quantum dots. For the N-RHCQDs, the main absorption peaks were found at 220 nm and 480 nm, which can be ascribed to the π–π* and n–π* transitions of the sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively [42]. As for the Bi-RHCQDs, the π–π* and n–π* transitions were determined at 220 nm and 400 nm of the sp2 hybridized carbon respectively [42]. Apart from that, it can be observed that the addition of dopants showed a red spectral shift towards a much more visible region. In the visible light region, for both N- and Bi-doping, further increment in the dopant amount caused the peaks to shift toward the blue region. The blue shift in the absorption peak can be explained by the quantum confinement effect upon the addition of more dopants [43]. At 800 nm, the absorption intensities of N-RHCQDs samples were lower than the Bi-RHCQDs samples. The reduction in the absorption intensities of the N-RHCQDs samples might be due the agglomeration, or aggregation in the N-RHCQDs. Agglomeration tends to reduce the light absorption by the nanoparticles, which led to the drop in the absorption intensity in the N-RHCQDs [44]. Fig. 4 (b) shows the band gap estimation of all the synthesized carbon quantum dots. Using the Tauc-plot method, the band gap of the samples was determined to be 2.15, 2.10 and 2.25 eV for N-RHCQDs (5 – 20 vol%) samples, and 2.90, 2.80 and 3.00 eV for Bi-RHCQDs (1–5 wt%) samples, respectively. The addition of dopants in the CQDs narrows the band gap energy in CQDs, which minimizes the recombination of electrons-holes pair in the CQDs. Further increment of amount of dopants gives a higher energy band gap, which means higher energy required for the electrons from valence band to move to conduction band of the CQDs, thus leading to recombination of electron-hole pairs in the CQDs [45].

Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of all the doped RHCQDs samples. Fig. 5 (b) shows the image of the doped RHCQDs samples under natural and UV light. When more EDA was added in the hydrothermal synthesis, the peaks of the PL intensity was much higher, and tended to red shift. EDA plays the role of a surface passivating agent in synthesis of carbon quantum dots and protecting the surface functional groups on the carbon quantum dots, thus giving a higher fluorescence intensity [46]. Furthermore, the greater amount of basic reagent will enhance the fluorescence intensity, as more negatively charged carbon quantum dots are produced due to the deprotonation process [47]. On the other hand, the addition of more bismuth (Bi) precursors in the hydrothermal synthesis also gave a much higher PL intensity. Bismuth, acting as metal dopant, can lead to a reduction in the band gap of the Bi-RHCQDs, causing a slight red shift in the peaks toward the visible light region [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point zero charge of doped RHCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Point zero charge (PZC) is the condition in which final pH is equal to the initial pH, whereby, the net charge on the surface of the material is zero [28]. In this study, the pHPZC of N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs were evaluated using a salt addition method. As shown in Fig. 6 , the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs gave a pHPZC of 8 and 6 respectively. The N-RHCQDs had a slightly higher value of pHPZC than Bi-RHCQDs, which was probably due to the higher electronegativity in the N-RHCQDs [49]. The pHPZC value plays an important role in validating the results of the effect of the pH on the photodegradation or adsorption. If the solution pH used is higher than pHPZC, the performance of photodegradation and adsorption will be enhanced due to the presence of much more negatively charges on the surface of catalyst or adsorbent, allowing the cationic charges in the substrate to bind with the catalyst, or adsorbent [50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of doping agents
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of all the doped RHCQDs samples were evaluated using the MB degradation under visible light irradiation over a period of 2 h using a fixed amount of doped RHCQDs and volume of MB. Fig. 7 (a) shows the degradation profile of the 10 ppm MB over both N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs. In the case of degradation of MB using the N-RHCQDs, 10 vol% of EDA was the optimum doping amount for N-RHCQDs, as it showed the highest degradation efficiency of 72.16%, after 2 h of irradiation under the 300 W Xenon lamp. On the other hand, for degradation of the MB over the Bi-RHCQDs, the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) gave the highest degradation efficiency of 68.91%, after 2 h of irradiation under the 300 W Xenon lamp. Generally, the degradation of MB over the doped RHCQDs happens through a radical mechanism. When the light is turned on, photons will excite the surface of the RHCQDs. Electrons (e-) in the valence band of the RHCQDs will leave the holes (h+) and move to the conduction band of the RHCQDs. The presence of surface functional groups on the RHCQDs traps the excited electrons-holes pairs and hinders the recombination. The electrons will react with the oxygen present in the solution, forming super oxide radicals (•O2 –). The vacant holes will react with the surface-adsorbed water to form hydroxyl radicals (•OH–). The two formed radicals are strong oxidizing agents, which tend to remove the MB in the solution [6,52,53]. The photocatalytic activity depends on the band gap of the carbon quantum dots. An efficient photocatalyst should exhibit band gap energy between 2.0 and 3.0 eV [54]. At larger band gap, the recombination of the electron-hole pairs will occur and lead to a much lower photocatalytic activity [55]. By introducing dopants into the CQDs, the dopants on the CQDs will tend to trap the excited carrier, hindering the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus leading to higher photocatalytic activities [53]. In the case of N-RHCQDs, the further addition of EDA (20 vol%) give a lower photocatalytic activity. High dopant amounts diminish the band gap of the CQDs, causing a blue shift in light absorption, leading to a much higher recombination [56]. Overall, it can be observed that N-RHCQDs perform better than Bi-RHCQDs. According to the study by Zhou et al., carbon quantum dots with narrow band gap will perform better in homogeneous phase photocatalytic reaction [57]. In this experiment, band gap energies of N-RHCQDs were much lower as compared to that of Bi-RHCQDs, thus N-RHCQDs showed better photodegradation performance.

The degradation profile of the MB by both the N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) was fitted onto pseudo-first and pseudo-second order kinetics, as shown in Fig. 7 (b) and (c). The degradation profiles of the MB by the N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) fitted well to the pseudo-second order kinetics, with R2 of 0.99 and 0.90, respectively, as compared to pseudo-first order kinetics which had R2 of 0.93 and 0.79, respectively. The pseudo-second order reaction implies that the MB degradation occurred in a chemisorption reaction between the MB and surface functional groups of the CQDs [58,59]. The degradation of MB over both the doped RHCQDs occurred at an almost similar rate. From the pseudo-second order kinetic plots, the rate constants for both the degradation using the N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) were calculated to be 0.0009 and 0.0008 L mg−1 min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration and initial pH
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of the initial concentration and the initial pH on the photodegradation of the MB were then tested using the two best samples, the N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%). The photodegradation profiles and kinetics of the MB degradation at different concentrations (5, 10, 15 ppm), and different initial pH values (2.5, 6.5 and 12.5), were shown in Fig. 8 . It can be observed that the degradation of the MB over the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs, worked well under lower concentration of 5 ppm, giving removal efficiencies of 95.39% and 89.31%, respectively. The degradation rate also became much faster under lower concentration, as shown in the rate constants of 0.012 and 0.006 L mg-1min−1. respectively. The photocatalytic activity is usually higher at a lower concentration of the substrate. At a higher concentration of MB, there will be much more substrate molecules adsorbed onto the surface of the carbon quantum dots, causing difficulty for the photon energy to reach the catalyst’s surface [60]. As a result, less ∙ OH radicals will be formed, which will result in a lower photodegradation efficiency.

On the other hand, in the study of the MB degradation over the doped RHCQDs at different pH values, it was observed that the MB degradation efficiencies were higher at a higher pH or basic condition, giving removal efficiencies of up to 82.02% and 76.42%, respectively. The degradation rate was also faster at the basic conditions, as shown in the kinetic plots, giving rate constants of 0.002 and 0.001 L mg-1min−1 respectively. The evaluation of the optimum pH for the degradation of the pollutant depends on the point zero charge, pHPZC of the photocatalyst and the surface charge of the pollutants. In this study, the pHPZC of the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs were pH 8 and pH 6 respectively. Therefore, when the pH is adjusted until its basic condition, the solution’s pH is higher than the pHPZC, and the abundant hydroxyl ions presented causes the carbon quantum dots to become negatively charged [61]. Since MB is a cationic dye, more MB will be adsorbed to the negatively charged carbon quantum dots [62].

The N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) which gave best performance in the dye degradation were selected for the performance testing of the copper (II) removal. The adsorption studies were done in a batch adsorption process. The effects of different parameters on the metal adsorption process, such as contact time, initial concentration and initial pH were observed. Fig. 9 (a) shows the influence of contact time on the Cu (II) removal performance. Initially, there was a dramatic increase in the removal percentage, as the Cu (II) ions slowly attached to the surface of the carbon quantum dots. At 10 min, there was a drop in removal performance, which might be due to the desorption process in the metal removal process. Studies by Tang et al. showed that adsorption and desorption can co-exist in the metal removal process due to a lack of adsorption sites, and low interaction between the adsorbates and the surface of the adsorbent [63]. At 60 min, the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs were able to remove 56.23% and 33.13% of Cu (II) ions, respectively. Following that, there was no further increment in removal percentage, as dynamic equilibrium was achieved, whereby all the available active sites on the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs had been occupied by the Cu (II) metal ions.

Fig. 9 (b) illustrates the effects of initial concentration of the Cu (II) solution on the Cu (II) removal performance of the doped RHCQDs. For both the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs, when the initial concentration of the copper solution increased from 10 ppm to 100 ppm, the removal percentage of the Cu (II) decreased from 56.23% to 13.52% for the N-RHCQDs, and 33.13% to 9.55% for the Bi-RHCQDs. At a higher concentration of Cu (II) solution, there were limited adsorption sites for the Cu (II) ions to dwell on, thus leading to a much lower removal efficiency upon increasing the initial concentration of the copper solution [64].

The effect of initial pH of copper solution on the Cu (II) removal performance is shown in Fig. 9 (c). At a higher pH or alkaline condition, the removal percentage of the Cu (II) increased up to 68.15% for the N-RHCQDs, and 38.02% for the Bi-RHCQDs. At a higher pH, the hydroxyl ions caused the carbon quantum dots to be much more electronegative, leading to a higher tendency to adsorb much more cationic copper (II) ions [24]. Overall, it can be observed that the N-RHCQDs had a greater Cu (II) removal performance than Bi-RHCQDs. The C-N bonding in the N-RHCQDs might have contributed to the high performance of the N-RHCQDs, as they made the N-RHCQDs much more electronegative, causing the Cu (II) ions to be easily adsorbed onto the surface [65].

The Cu (II) removal by N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) fits well the pseudo-second kinetics model well, with a R2 of 1.00 and 0.99 respectively as shown in Fig. 10 (a). The linear equation of the pseudo-second kinetics model is expressed as below:

The pseudo-second kinetics model implies that the adsorption of the Cu (II) onto the surface of the doped RHCQDs takes place in the chemisorption process, whereby the chemical bonding is formed between the Cu (II) ions, and the surface of the doped RHCQDs [63,66]. The kinetic parameters,

were calculated to be 0.56 g/mg.min and 2.22 mg/g for the N-RHCQDs; 0.54 g/mg.min and 1.29 mg/g for the Bi-RHCQDs, hereby implying that the adsorption rate of the N-RHCQDs was much faster than that of the Bi-RHCQDs. Fig. 10 (b) shows the graph for the adsorption capacity against the initial concentration of copper solution. In this study, the highest adsorption capacity was found to be 5.41 and 3.81 mg/g for the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs, respectively.

The interaction and binding between the adsorbate and adsorbent were studied by fitting the data to the Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm models respectively as shown in equations (4) and (5) below:

The results of the corresponding isotherms’ parameters are summarized in Table 2 . As shown in Fig. 10 (d) and Table 2, the results suggests that the adsorption process of the Cu (II) ions onto the surface of the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs fits the Langmuir isotherm better, giving a R2 of 0.9318 and 0.9913, respectively. The Langmuir isotherm suggests that the adsorption mechanism of the Cu (II) ions onto surface of doped RHCQDs is that of a monolayer adsorption [66]. The maximum adsorption uptake of the Cu (II) ions onto both the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs was determined to be 5.73 and 4.08 mg/g respectively. The maximum adsorption capacity in this study was comparable with that of several previous studies, as shown in Table 3 .

In conclusion, this study reported on the effect of nitrogen and bismuth doping towards the performance of the rice husk-derived carbon quantum dots in photodegradation of methylene blue, as well as that of copper (II) removal from aqueous solution. The addition of EDA, and bismuth precursors in the carbon quantum dots shifted the emission wavelength to a much more visible light region. It is proven that photoluminescence of the CQDs is tunable under different amounts of nitrogen and bismuth dopants. In the photodegradation of MB, the N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and the Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) gives the highest degradation efficiency of 72.16% and 68.91% respectively. The degradation of methylene blue by the N-RHCQDs (10 vol%) and Bi-RHCQDs (5 wt%) fit well with the pseudo-second kinetic model, with a kinetic constant of 0.0009 and 0.0008 L mg−1 min−1. The two doped RHCQDs were then tested for copper (II) removal, which gave a removal performance of 56.23% and 33.13%, respectively. The kinetic and isotherm studies suggest that the Cu (II) adsorption onto the N-RHCQDs and Bi-RHCQDs obey the pseudo-second kinetic model and Langmuir isotherm, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 5.73 and 4.08 mg/g respectively. Therefore, the work proved the effectiveness of using N-doped and Bi-doped rice husk-derived carbon quantum dots as catalyst or adsorbent for dye degradation, and heavy metal removal from polluted water.

Khee Chung Hui: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wei Lun Ang: Supervision, Methodology, Resources. Nonni Soraya Sambudi: Writing - review & editing, Resources, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
